abstract: Traditional cryogenic distillation for noble gas separation remains prohibitively expensive and energy-intensive, particularly for dilute gas streams. While metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) functionalized with polarizing groups are theoretical frontrunners for solid-state Xe/Kr separation, experimental evidence clarifying the precise nature of the Xe–framework interaction has remained elusive. In this work, we present SBMOF-2, a novel microporous framework that exhibits high selectivity for Xe over Kr under ambient conditions. At 298 K, SBMOF-2 achieves a Xe/Kr selectivity of 10 and a significant xenon uptake capacity of 27.07 wt%. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction reveals that this selectivity originates from the unique pore architecture: a series of cages lined with phenyl rings and polar hydroxyl groups that cooperatively function as high-affinity adsorption sites for the polarizable xenon atoms. We further validated the material's industrial viability through experimental breakthrough curves and computational modeling, both of which demonstrated sharp Kr-then-Xe elution profiles. Our results indicate that the adsorption capacity and selectivity of SBMOF-2 are on par with top-tier benchmarks like Ni-MOF-74 and cobalt formate, establishing it as a highly effective candidate for practical noble gas purification.
